text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the omentum adhered to the sub-serous fibroid. A small intramural fibroid is visible on the anterior wall of the uterus but is not removed. The pedunculated sub-serous fibroid is carefully excised from its base using the harmonic scalpel without the need for vasopressin.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the fibroid, avoiding the bladder located just behind it. The fibroid is then removed and placed aside for later morcellation.

The surgeon identifies the dermoid cyst in the left ovary, noting the normal appearance of the right ovary. The cyst is visually tough, and care is taken to avoid rupture during laparoscopic separation.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue, breaking hydrogen bonds to achieve a clear plane of dissection.

The surgeon uses an endo bag to remove the dermoid cyst, holding it with a grasper. Upon grasping, the cyst ruptures, and suction is immediately applied to extract the fluid from the dermoid. The ovary is visible below the cyst, and the entire dermoid is separated.

The surgeon transfers the dermoid cyst and surrounding fatty material into an endo bag, which is then removed through a 10mm cannula. Following the removal, a thorough lavage is performed with approximately two liters of saline, ensuring the patient's head is in reverse Trendelenburg position to prevent fluid from trickling into the upper abdomen.

The surgeon performs repeated lavage to wash out fluid content and fatty material from the surgical field, ensuring thorough suction of all remnants. The fibroid is visible and will be removed later through morcellation. Irrigation is restarted, with fluid poured between the coils of the intestine, using a high flow insufflator during suction.

The surgeon uses an electrical morcellator from Ethicon Gynecare to morcellate the fibroid, while the left ovary floats in the surgical field. The remaining spilled content is being sucked out, with the uterus visible below.
